entries.title: Command palette - quick launch of commands from the keyboard
entries.body:
# Command palette

The **Command palette** is a keyboard-driven panel for quick access to all of Burp's functionality. In spirit it is the same "command palette" found in modern IDEs and editors like VS Code: you open it with a single combination, start typing the name of the action you need - and the list filters on the fly down to the matching commands, which you can run right away without digging through menus.

## Why you need it

Burp's interface is large: a multitude of tabs, tools, settings entries. Hunting for what you need with the mouse through menus is slow. The command palette solves this: instead of "recalling where a function lives and clicking your way to it" you simply type its name. This noticeably speeds up everyday work and helps you discover capabilities you might not have known about - just type a keyword and see what Burp suggests.

## How to open it

The main way is the hotkey **Ctrl+K** (on macOS - **Cmd+K**). If this combination is taken by another action on your system, it can be reassigned: **Settings > User interface > Hotkeys**.

## What you can do via the palette

The palette is a single entry point to Burp's varied actions. Through it, by typing a name, you can:

* **Navigate between tools and tabs** - quickly switch to the tool you need.
* **Run actions and commands** - perform operations without opening the corresponding menu.
* **Open settings** - jump to the configuration sections you need.
* **Work with extensions** - including invoking commands added by installed extensions, and accessing the BApp Store.
* **Run a search across the project** (in the Professional edition).

Some commands are **context-dependent**: they appear in the palette only when the appropriate tool is active or the appropriate item is selected.

## How search works

As you type, the command list **filters dynamically** in real time, showing matches by partial name (you do not need to type the whole name). Navigation is familiar: arrows select a command, Enter runs it. Recently used commands are shown higher up, so frequently needed actions are at hand.

## Editions

The command palette is available in both **Community Edition** and **Professional**. Individual actions invoked from it (for example, the project search) remain features of the corresponding edition.

## Practice

* **Make Ctrl+K a reflex.** Need to go somewhere or run something - hit the palette and type, it is almost always faster than the mouse.
* **Use it as a "capability navigator".** Do not remember the exact name or where a function lives - type a keyword (for example, "scan", "scope", "decode") and see what Burp suggests.
* **Reassign the hotkey on a conflict.** If **Ctrl+K** is intercepted by something else, do not give up on the palette - set your own combination in **Settings > User interface > Hotkeys**.

entries.title: Search - cross-tool search over Burp's data
entries.body:
# Search (cross-tool search)

**Search** is a cross-tool search over Burp's data: it looks for given text or a pattern across several tools at once - in HTTP messages from the proxy history, in Repeater, in the site map (Target), and so on. It is a way to quickly answer the question "where in all the collected traffic does this string appear?" - for example, a specific parameter, token, or leaked secret.

## Why you need it

Over an active test Burp accumulates thousands of requests and responses scattered across different tools. Finding all occurrences of a needed value among them by hand is impossible. The cross-tool **Search** does this centrally: one query - and before you are all the places where what you are looking for appears, with the ability to jump straight to each one.

Typical tasks:

* find **all** requests/responses where a particular parameter appears (for example, `redirect_url` or `token`);
* hunt through all the traffic for leaks of sensitive data (passwords, keys, internal addresses);
* find all responses referring to a specific URL/resource.

## How to open it

The main way is to click **Search** on Burp's tab bar: the cross-tool search tool will open. Additionally, search over a specific branch of the site map is available from the context menu: right-click in the site map > **Engagement tools > Search**. The search has no dedicated default hotkey, but it is easy to invoke via the command palette (**Ctrl+K** -> type "search") or to assign your own hotkey in **Settings > User interface > Hotkeys**.

## Search options

The search is flexibly configurable:

* **Search expression** - the string you are looking for; can be either a literal or a **regular expression (Regex)**.
* **Case sensitive** - take case into account or ignore it.
* **Negative search** - find items that, on the contrary, do **not** contain the expression.
* **Restrict to scope** - search only among in-scope items.
* **Where to search in the message** - for example, in the headers or in the request/response body.
* **Which tools** - restrict the search to the selected tools (Target, Proxy, Repeater, Organizer).
* **Dynamic update** - results pick up new traffic as it arrives, so the search stays current right during testing.

## How to read the results

What is found is shown as a **table**: source (which tool produced the item), host (protocol + host name), URL (path and query), as well as response code, length, time. Below the table is a **preview panel** with the full request and response of the selected row, and **the matches themselves are highlighted**, so what you are looking for is immediately visible in context. Clicking a row opens the corresponding message for detailed viewing.

Nearby are the adjacent operations from the engagement tools: **Find references** (find responses referring to the selected resource), **Find comments**, **Find scripts** (search for comments and scripts in site map branches).

## Editions

The cross-tool **Search** (and the related engagement tools) belongs to **Burp Suite Professional**.

## Practice

* **Search for secrets across all the traffic.** You suspect a leak of a token/key - run it through Search: you will find all responses where it showed up.
* **Map a parameter.** To understand where a parameter of interest is used, find all its occurrences and jump through the results to specific requests.
* **Narrow by scope and tools.** On a large project, restrict the search to in-scope and the tools you need - less noise, faster results.
* **Regex for complex patterns.** Enable **Regex** mode when you are looking not for an exact string but for a pattern (a token format, a key mask, and the like).

entries.title: Context menu (right click)
entries.body:
# Context menu

The **Context menu** is the right-click menu available throughout Burp: on HTTP messages and in tables (Proxy > HTTP history, the site map in Target, Repeater, Logger, Scanner results, and so on). It is the workhorse of the interface: it is precisely from here that requests are thrown between tools, scope is managed, findings are marked, and analysis is launched. The menu is "smart" - it shows only those actions that are appropriate for the selected item.

## Sending to other tools (Send to ...)

The most frequent group is forwarding the selected request further down the pipeline:

* **Send to Repeater** (**Ctrl+R**) - manual editing and resending (checking for injections, IDOR, logic).
* **Send to Intruder** (**Ctrl+I**) - automated brute-forcing and fuzzing.
* **Send to Sequencer** - analysis of token randomness.
* **Send to Comparer** - compare with another message.
* **Send to Organizer** - set an interesting request aside for later.
* **Send to Decoder** - parse/assemble data in various encodings.

## Scanning (Professional)

For active testing, the scanner is launched from the menu:

* **Do active scan** - active scanning of the selected item.
* **Do passive scan** - passive analysis.
* **Scan defined insertion points** - scan the defined insertion points.

These items are **Professional** features.

## Engagement tools (Professional)

The engagement tools submenu gathers recon and auxiliary operations:

* **Search** - cross-tool search over the selected item/branch.
* **Find references** - find responses referring to the item.
* **Find comments**, **Find scripts** - search for comments and scripts.
* **Discover content** - discovery of unlinked content/functionality.
* **Analyze target** - a summary of the target (static/dynamic URLs, parameters).
* **Schedule task** - schedule an automatic task.
* **Generate CSRF PoC** - generate an HTML PoC for CSRF.
* **Simulate manual testing** - generate traffic that imitates manual testing.

These are also **Professional** capabilities.

## Scope management and navigation

* **Add to scope** / **Remove from scope** - add/remove from the testing scope.
* **Add to site map** - add to the site map.
* **Go to issue** - jump to the related finding (where applicable).

## Copying and the clipboard

* **Copy URL** - copy the URL.
* **Copy as curl command** - copy the request as a `curl` command (handy for reproducing outside Burp and for reports).
* **Copy links** - copy links from the selection.
* **Copy to file** / **Paste from file** - export to a file / paste from a file.

## Request and response in the browser

* **Request in browser** - replay the request in the browser: *in the original session* (in original session) or *in the current browser session* (in current browser session).
* **Show response in browser** - open the response in a real browser (Burp issues a one-time URL for this) - handy to see how the response actually renders.

## Annotations and other

* **Add comment** / **Highlight** - a note and colour highlighting of a row.
* **Change request method**, **Change body encoding** - quick transformations of the request.
* **Save item** / export - save the selected item.

## Items from extensions

Installed extensions add **their own** items to the context menu. Technically, an extension implements a menu item factory (via the Montoya API / the deprecated `IContextMenuFactory`) and, based on the invocation context (which tool, which messages/findings are selected, which text fragment is selected), returns the appropriate actions. So the set of menu items also depends on which extensions you have installed.

## Practice

* **Learn the hotkeys for frequent actions.** **Ctrl+R** (to Repeater) and **Ctrl+I** (to Intruder) save a mass of clicks - this is the main route of a request between tools.
* **Copy as curl - for reports and sharing.** A quick way to hand a colleague or paste into a ticket an exact reproducible request.
* **Show response in browser** - when you need to understand how the response looks "through the browser's eyes" (for example, when assessing reflected XSS).
* **Do not forget the Engagement tools.** During recon, **Find references**, **Discover content**, and **Generate CSRF PoC** noticeably speed up the routine (available in Professional).

entries.title: Filter settings - display filters in tables
entries.body:
# Filter settings (display filters)

**Display filters** control which of the **already captured** items are **shown** in Burp's tables - in Proxy > HTTP history, in the site map (Target), in the WebSockets history, and others. The key point: a filter **does not delete anything** and does not affect what is captured - it merely temporarily hides some rows from view. Remove the filter and what was hidden comes back. (The exception is **Logger**, where there is a separate capture filter that actually decides what to record; see the section on Logger.)

## Why you need it

The history quickly swells to thousands of entries, and what you need drowns among them. The display filter narrows the table down to the relevant: only in-scope, only with parameters, only certain response codes, and so on. This is not about "throwing data away" but about "focusing the gaze" - at any moment the conditions can be changed without losing anything.

## Filtering criteria

The set of conditions differs slightly between tools, but the basis is common. For **HTTP history** the typical composition is:

* **By request type:** show only **in-scope** items; only items **with responses** (hide those without a response); only requests **with parameters** (parameterized).
* **By response MIME type:** show only the selected content types (HTML, XML, scripts, images, and so on).
* **By status code:** show only the selected response codes.
* **By search term:** keep items containing a given string; a literal or **Regex** is supported, as well as case sensitivity (**Case sensitive**) and **Negative search** (show items that did NOT match).
* **By file extension:** show/hide by extension (for example, only `php`, hide `css`/`js`).
* **By annotation:** show only marked items (with a comment or highlight).
* **By listener:** show items that arrived on a specific proxy listener port.

## Specifics by tool

* **Site map.** Additionally there is tree control - for example, **hide empty folders** (hide empty folders) when, because of the filter, a branch is left without visible child items. Search by term in the site map belongs to **Professional**.
* **WebSockets history.** The filters are tailored to message direction: show only incoming/outgoing, only in-scope; there is search by term and a filter by annotations/listener. There is essentially no MIME type here.

## How to open and apply

1. Click the **filter bar** above the table/tree - the filter settings window will open.
2. Configure the criteria: in **Settings mode** - with checkboxes and lists; or switch to **Script mode** and describe the logic with a script (**Bambdas**, in Java) for arbitrary filtering.
3. Changes are applied immediately; a quick toggle lets you temporarily enable/disable the filter without resetting the settings.

A convenient trick for learning scripts: set up the filter with checkboxes, then use **Convert to script** - Burp will translate your conditions into a script that you can then refine by hand.

## Filter, scope, and Logger - do not confuse them

* **Display filter vs Scope.** Scope sets the **boundaries of the target** (what you are testing at all). The filter narrows the **display** of what has already been collected. They are combined: for example, "only in-scope" + "only with parameters" to see only the interactive points of your target.
* **Display filter vs the Logger capture filter.** In **Logger** there are two filters: the **Capture filter** actually decides what to **record** (what is discarded is lost and saves memory), while the view filter decides what to **show**. The ordinary display filter in HTTP history/site map is always non-destructive: it only hides, never deletes.

## Editions

Basic filtering is available in both editions. Some capabilities - the search-term filter (in a number of places) and advanced **Bambdas** scripts - belong to **Professional**.

## Practice

* **Focus on the target.** The combination "in-scope + parameterized" cuts off static content and third-party noise, leaving what is actually worth testing.
* **Several windows - several views.** You can open several history/site map windows with different filters and look at the traffic from different angles simultaneously.
* **Mark first, then filter.** Highlight/comment + a filter by annotation = a quick return to previously marked interesting requests.
* **Do not cut away anything destructively.** Remember: the display filter is safe (nothing is lost). But in **Logger** be careful with the **Capture filter** - what is discarded there cannot be recovered.
